Blue Moon
Composed by Richard Rodgers
Lyrics by Lorenz Hart

Toreador Aria Song from "Carmen"
Composed by Georges Bizet

If I Had Words
Adapted from "Symphony No. 3" by Camille Saint-Saëns
Composed by Jonathan Hodge

Cantique De Jean Racine
Composed by Gabriel Fauré
Performed by Celeste Lazarenko and Mariel Pazin
With Louise Johnson (Harp) and Simon Kenway (Conductor)

Jingle Bells
Composed by James Pierpont

Blu-ray Special Features and Extras:

Special Feature: The Making of ‘BABE’ [1995] [480i] [1.37:1] [3:56] Despite this very short brief feature, here we have a very interesting look at the Early Tests of making the pig talk with the computer modelling necessary to achieve the mouth movements and facial expressions for the animals. Rhythm and Hues Studios was an American visual effects and animation company that received the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects in 1995 for ‘BABE,’ and in 2008 for ‘The Golden Compass,’ and in 2013 for ‘Life of Pi.’ It has also received four Scientific and Technical Academy Awards and is the CGI animation company that augmented real and animatronic animals to make them speak. Several shots of Babe, the sheep dogs and the sheep speaking are shown in their original state, and then we see all the farm animals turned into a computer generated image. Sadly in 2013 they went out of business.

Special Feature: George Miller on ‘BABE’ [2003] [480i] [1.37:1] [6:12] Here we are introduced to George Miller, who was the Producer of the film ‘BABE,’ plus Bill Miller and Doug Mitchell, as well as George Miller and also writing partner Chris Noonan. This feature has George Miller talking about his pet project and makes for a nice companion piece to the audio commentary and throughout this feature we get headings explaining his thought about the film in general and we get a nice lot of clips from the film, with the equally wonderful film composed music. George Miller actually covers a lot more ground than you might think. We get a heading “Where Does Babe Come From?” George Miller starts by recounting on how he first encountered Dick King-Smith’s novel, from the description he heard while flying over to London in 1986. I believe this would be during the time he was finishing the film ‘The Witches of Eastwick,’ and George Miller says that he heard a female BBC announcer who could not stop giggling while trying to describe the novel “Babe” as the best family reading of that prior year. After George Miller reading the book in his hotel room, he immediately determined to make the film and decided to delay the process until after ‘Jurassic Park’ proved that CGI had advanced far enough to make the talking animals possible in a manner that wouldn’t look like Muppets even though Jim Henson’s group did create the animatronic characters for their film. George Miller also relates some other very funny tales, including an argument he had with Tom Pollock, then the Vice-Chair of Universal Pictures about how his unprejudiced film was making a villain out of the only cat in the story. So, George Miller had them add a note into the narration that there are bad apples everywhere, and this specific cat just happens to be a bad apple. George Miller talks about the actors in the film, and especially James Cromwell having to work with the little pig and not having to say a lot of dialogue. George talks about the “Babes Voice” and whether to use a child or an adult’s voice and happen to come across Christine Cavanaugh (pigs voice), who he felt was ideal. With the heading “All Cats Are Not Bad” and talks about he used to be when he was a child would split people up into two groups who were either dog lovers or cat lovers, whereas George was definitely a dog lover. With the heading “Pigs Are definitely Smart” and since directing ‘BABE’ is totally abhorrent at the thought of eating any part of the pig, as he thinks they are totally adorable, and are just as intelligent as dogs. With the heading “A Good Heart” George talks about the outline of the story and the ability of courage, especially with Babe the pig itself, who he felt had such a good heart. So all in all this was a very nice little feature. One element notably missing from this interview is any discussion of the work with Chris Noonan.

Feature Audio Commentary with Screenwriter/Producer George Miller: Here George Miller introduces himself and mentions that when you see the credits, he informs us that he was one of the producers and screenwriter with this film, and when you see “A Kennedy Miller Film,” George mentions that it was a company he founded with another partner years ago. George felt with the film ‘BABE’ that he wanted it to feel like “story book” scenario and when you see all the artefacts on the wall you see as the camera pans past them, George felt it gave a real feel to what the film was all about, as well as making the film like a fable. George talks extensively about the composer Nigel Westlake, who he found to be a very talented person and felt he was so totally ideal for his film, as a previous composer let George down. When you see inside the horrible pig rearing farm that is permanently in the dark and where you see the mother sows cruelly carted off to be slaughtered and Babe crying for his mother, George says that the problem of the shooting over a thirteen week period, young pigs grow up very fast and that caused lots of problems with continuity, so of course they had a rotation of young pigs on hand, so you think they are the same original young pigs. When Farmer Hoggett [James Cromwell] first meets Babe at the Fayre, George felt that from then on a bond was made between farmer and pig, with an eventual common destiny together, where they change the world, and of course it is a classic hero story, especially towards Babe. When you see all the different headings that appear at certain points in the film, well this came about after the film was shot and Margaret Sixel, the South African-born film editor, who of course had to view the film while editing it, felt it was too episodic in style, and so “Chapter Headings” were suggested to be inserted into the final print and George felt this helped to make the film more appealing and to help with each storyline chapters in the film, it also helped when they did a film preview with young children, because when it was shown the age group could not read at the time and so the parents had to inform their children what the words said, and so that is how the three small mice were introduced so they could speak the words of the “Chapter Headings.” When you get to seeing the sheep dog puppies being sold, you actually get to see the animal trainer Karl Lewis Miller (1941 – 2008) who was the animal trainer for the film ‘BABE,’ who was a very successful and renowned animal trainer, and had trained thousands of animals were trained by him for their roles in feature films or TV shows, and especially with the famous Doberman scenes in 'REMO WILLIAMS’ [1985] who were brilliantly choreographed and to make it really something special, as he got 3 Doberman Pinchers to work in close consultation together and this was because he did outstanding professional work as an action animal trainer at its best, who is now very sadly missed. George points out as we get to the scene where Babe has to do the sheep dog trials, all the strands of the fable story are coming together and especially when it comes to grumpy sheep dog Rex who now really wants to help Babe, and most importantly the sheep dog trials is now having the rules turned upside down. When we get to near the very end of the film, George talks about where James Cornwall is supposedly talking to Babe, but instead is talking at the lens of the camera, and George tells us that instead he is talking like as though he was actually talking to his Father, and George felt that was a very moving experience and when you hear the narrator Roscoe Lee Browne speaks the final words, these are the actual passage at the end of the novel “Babe” by Dick King-Smith. So all in all this is really fascinating and interesting audio commentary by George Miller, and especially going into the nuts and bolts of blending the animatronics, robots and CGI elements with the live animals, as well as the occasional human wrangling. So a definite audio commentary to listen to, as you learn so much on the process of how the film ‘BABE’ came about and especially all the trials and tribulations, which again is a totally fascinating listen, as you really get the feel that George Miller really loved and enjoyed the process of making this heart-warming film and bringing it to the screen for everyone to see and enjoy.

Special Feature: pocket BLU app: The Blu-ray comes with pocket BLU app functionality for those viewers who want to make use of the appropriate smartphone, iPhone, iPad and Android applications. With these features you will be able to stream content and communicate with ease!

Special Feature: BD-Live: To view the contents via your Blu-ray player, it has to be connected to the internet; otherwise it is not BD-LIVE capable. For possible solutions to resolve this problem, please consult your Blu-ray player manual.

Finally, ‘BABE’ [1995] is not merely a treat for the eyes. The film's blending of real animals and computer-animated puppetry is seamless to the point that one hardly notices it is computer generated. And the actor’s voices with the farm animals and domestic pets give then distinctive personalities are well chosen and imaginative. Particularly evocative are Hugo Weaving as Rex, a proud, short-tempered sheepdog with a hearing disability; Evelyn Krape as Old Ewe, the fussy but good-hearted Grande dame of the farm's flock of sheep, and Danny Mann as Ferdinand, a cheeky duck who dreams of being a rooster. ‘BABE’ is one fantastic Blu-ray that collectors will definitely want to own especially for its impressive image quality. ‘BABE’ on this Blu-ray disc is not just for the young but the young at heart. All in all, this is a really wonderful and fantastic film, that really gladdens the heart, but will also bring an emotional of joy to you, but be prepared to get the Kleenex tissues out, as you will definitely will shed a few tears of joyous happiness. This is a definite one to add to your Blu-ray Collection. Highly Recommended!

Andrew C. Miller – Your Ultimate No.1 Film Aficionado
Le Cinema Paradiso
United Kingdom
rows.title: BABE [1995 / 2011] [Blu-ray]

rows.text:
I don't know why His Master's Voice made this recording in the first place. After all Menuhin had made a stereo recording of Beethoven's Violin Concerto in 1960 with the Vienna Philharmonic under Constantin Silvestri, in excellent stereo sound and a publication which had been received with accolades in The Gramophone ([[ASIN:B00005UUOE Beethoven: Violin Concerto / Romances No. 1 & 2]]). So why re-do it six years later? And I think, in those pre-Naxos years, they even PAID the performers that they recorded, not just royalties on sales, but upfront, and hefty sums too when they were stars like these. Maybe EMI (the owning company of HMV) was hoping to bank on two of their greatest stars and on the universal success of Klemperer's complete Beethoven symphonies, recorded in the late 1950s to early 1960s. And Klemp' had never recorded Beethoven's Violin Concerto (he would do the complete Piano Concertos a year later with Barenboim - and there are no recordings left of him, studio or live, in the Triple Concerto).

If so, it misfired. The recording was generally received with more than reluctance, and to this day it appears to be always dismissed in favor of Menuhin's earlier versions, especially those with Furtwängler, the more famous one with the Philharmonia Orchestra from 1953 ([[ASIN:B000002SG3 Beethoven, Mendelssohn: Violin Concertos (Violin Concerto in D Major, Op. 61 / Violin Concerto in E Minor, Op. 64)]]) and, now that it has been reissued and is easily accessible on CD, their earlier recording from Lucerne in 1947 ([[ASIN:B000003XKA Beethoven: Violin Concerto; Romances Nos. 1 & 2]]).

The review of the original release published in the Gramophone issue of November 1966 by Edward Greenfield is very typical and deserves to be reproduced (and if you find that it strangely resembles the review posted by Colloredo of Caracas, don't infer that Edward Greenfield is still alive and living in Venezuela, but rather that Colloredo helped himself in the Gramophone electronic archive without citing his source):

"The point is that the special individual qualities of Menuhin and Furtwängler matched one another. Each brought out the best in the other, for their basic approach was identical, profoundly romantic and expressive but with an inner spiritual strength that was more elusive in emerging. I was hoping that the equally interesting combination of Menuhin and Klemperer would precipitate equally fine results, but I am afraid there is nothing like the same marriage of minds. One might have hoped that Menuhin's spontaneous imagination would lighten the impact of Klemperer and that Klemperer would give extra weight to Menuhin, but it has not worked that way. Admittedly Menuhin seems a degree or two more relaxed than he did in the Silvestri version made in Vienna, and the intonation is unquestionably better, but in neither case does that say very much. Though naturally there are many points of detail to admire, the overall impression is very much on the heavy side. One finds oneself objecting to the slow speed for the first movement, only to find that the basic tempo of both the earlier versions is a degree slower still. The fault lies here, I think, mainly with Klemperer, and no passage illustrates this more clearly than the beginning of the first movement coda after the cadenza. The pizzicato accompanying chords sound heavy not because they are loud or weighty but simply because they drag after the beat and the soloist seems to be pulling the players along.

In the slow movement Menuhin finds the mood of relaxation that was so conspicuously lacking in the Silvestri version, but go back to the Furtwängler and each phrase has a rapt sense of magic, of being created spontaneously in a way that makes the new version seem a little ordinary. In the finale the Silvestri version takes a turn for the better with Menuhin gaining confidence and a genuine spring in the rhythm. The new one is the slowest of the three, and characteristically Klemperer refuses to put a spring in the rhythm at all, so that at first it seems dull after the other two. But taken on its won it has a good bucolic solidity, and the middle D minor episode is firmer and stronger than in either of the previous versions. And interesting Klemperer point in the coda: instead of playing the emphatic crotchet plus quaver chordes at an even volume, he plays the last two comparatively quietly to allow the soloist's rising figure to come trhough."

And upon the first CD reissue, on EMI's mid-price Studio collection, in August 1985, Ivan March:

"Sir Yehudi Menuhin's recording of Beethoven's Violin Concerto dates from a decade earlier (1966) and does not show this artist at his best. Clearly there was no symbiosis in his partnership with Klemperer and when the slolo playing is sometimes rough in timbre, to the point of edginess, this is difficult to enjoy. The sound is brightly lit and not flattering. As it happens Menuhin's two earlier recordings have both been recently reissued. The 1954 mono performance with Furtwängler has been given CD status and was warmly welcomed by MEO, but more comparable in price is the Eminence reissue of the 1960 stereo version with a splendid accompaniment from the Vienna Philharmonic under Silvestri, which is in every way preferable to the later account".

Yet I find this Menuhin-Klemperer version much better than its reputation.

First, the notion that Klemperer conducts slow, let alone TOO slow, must be seriously qualified. It is true that he takes slightly longer to reach the first movement cadenza than Furtwängler in 1953 (20:12 vs 19:49) or 1947 in Lucerne (19:44) and significantly longer than Silvestri (19:14). But in fact, for the first part of the first movement, up until the re-entry of the violin on its opening rising motive, Klemperer's timing is EXACTLY the same as Furtwängler's: 10:40 in Lucerne, 10:50 with the Philharmonia, 10:50 with Klemperer. So it's only thereafter that Furwängler, in typical Furtwängler fashion, tends to step up his tempo, while Klemperer, in typical Klemperer fashion, remains steady as a rock.

Greenfeld's review seems serious enough, but when you listen to the recordings you realize that much of it is crap. "The basic tempo of both the earlier versions is a degree slower still"? (and here Greenfield is referring, of course, to Furtwängler 1953 and Silvestri 1960, not to Furtwängler 1947). No it's not! Basic tempo of Furtwängler 1953 was exactly the same (he reached the end of the orchestral introduction in 3:22, to Klemperer's... 3:20), and basic tempo of Silvestri in 1960 was significantly faster (though not really in the overture, which he conducted in 3:17). Precisely, one of the main differences between the Silvestri and the Klemperer version, is that the first remains very strict in tempo even in the more lyrical passages and Menuhin deploys his lyricism within the discipline of that strict tempo, while the other takes the time to relax and let the violin melodies soar more freely (that's what Greenfield alludes to when he writes, with justification, that "Menuhin seems a degree or two more relaxed than he did in the Silvestri version made in Vienna").

Not that this invalidates Greenfield's contention that, overall, Klemperer conducts a slower version; in fact, it reinforces the notion. That said, such expansive tempos may have seemed more shocking to some ears in 1966 than they do now. Prior to Klemperer, very few recordings had crossed the 20-minute threshold to reach the cadenza. Schneiderhan and Van Kempen were just there in 1953 (20:02), Oistrakh was way over with Cluytens in 1958 (20:42), Ferras-Sargent took 20:10 in 1959, Kogan-Silvetri 20:21 the same year, and Suk-Konwitschny had broken the 21-minute barrier in 1962 (21:10). But Menuhin-Klemperer seemed to have opened the dams. Came Ferras-Karajan in 1967 (21:07), Suk-Boult in 1970 (21:22), Zukerman-Barenboim in 1977 (20:35). When the famed version of Perlman-Guilini came out in 1981, it was hailed in The Gramophone ("no finer version available", they said - which of course left all those that were no more available) and the reviewer didn't complain that its first movement was too slow, although they reached the cadenza in 20:17. By the way that reviewer was Edward Greenfield.

Of course there is more to the feeling of slowness than just timing. But precisely, the claim against Klemperer and his purported slowness leaves entirely aside the very distinctive feature of his art of conducting, which makes him one of the greats of the 20th century: not just the deliberation, but the massive power he invests in the orchestra. And that's one massive advantage over Furtwängler's 1953 version, where by dint of the sonics but also the interpretation the orchestral tutti are much mellower. I hear none of the heaviness pointed by Greenfeld, because other than investing the tutti with massive power, Klemperer always keeps the phrasings crisp and accented. To me this doesn't sound "slow" or "heavy", it sounds immensely powerful.

But how could Greenfeld have heard that? At the end of his review of the Klemperer version, he confesses that he's "only heard the mono as yet"! Jesus!!!! And you are going to call a Rembrandt a fake based on a black and white photo??? Critics...

In fact, there are many advantages of this first movement with Klemperer over the two previous ones: over Furtwängler 1953, the stereo sonics and the sense of orchestral power; over Silvestri, the greater flexibility of tempo and greater songfulness in the lyrical passages. And to that, you may add that the 1966 sonics are an improvement, in clarity in impact, over even those of 1960.

And that thing of Greenfield with the pizzicati after the cadenza (23:09): frankly, I don't know what he is talking about, what he's heard or thought he'd heard - maybe again an effect of listening to the mono recording? There's no dragging of Klemperer here, there is the customary slow and meditative going out of the cadenza, and on the contrary as soon as the bassoon re-enters at 23:51, Klemperer adopts a rather flowing tempo where others prefer to retain the very expansive mood of meditativeness.

More crap from Greenfield: that Menuhin's "intonation is unquestionably better" than in 1960 with Silvestri. Obviously Ivan March heard the opposite. To my ears they are both wrong. Certainly the tone is not luminous as it was in 1947, there are a few edgy moments verging on wiriness in the runs and double stops of the Finale, Menuhin enters in Kreisler's first movement cadenza with more gentleness and less vibrancy than in 1960 (although this may be only the effect of a slightly more distant microphone placement), but you can find the same kind of - truly minuscule - tonal blemishes already in 1960 and even in 1953, and they don't add up to anything near the painful listening experience described by March. In fact, looking for tonal differences or trying to check on some I think I've heard, I am jumping from one version to the others, and the more carefully I listen and compare, the more, like the legendary desert mirage, they seem to elude me. Truth is, contrary to reputation (and sometimes fact), there is NO notable tonal deterioration between 1953, 1960 and 1966. The only implicit pointer that there might have been some technical deterioration in the 1966 Menuhin is that he plays a much-abridged version of the Kreisler 3rd movement cadenza, a decision for which I can find no obvious rationale: certainly not timing constraints of an LP side: even with the Cadenza played complete, Side B wouldn't have been longer than side A. This is not the greatest and purest violin tone in the world, but it is nothing to rant about and more than acceptable (I find the much-hailed Szigeti much more painful to hear in his 1947 recording with Bruno Walter, to say nothing of his 1961 remake with Dorati), and Menuhin deploys his customary lyricism and songfulness in the upper reaches.

"In the slow movement Menuhin finds the mood of relaxation that was so conspicuously lacking in the Silvestri version"? Crap again. With Klemperer the slow movement takes 10:09 to unfold (up to the cadential flourish leading to the Finale), and it was 10:13 with Silvestri, both find the very same "mood of relaxation", which is in fact a return to the way Menuhin did it with Furtwängler in 1947 (10:37). In fact Silvestri is even slightly MORE spacious than Klemperer in the first part of the movement, it is only with the movingly hushed passage with "raindrop" pzzicatti accompaniment (reached in 6:00 to Silvestri's 6:16) that Menuhin-Klemperer are now slightly more held-back. It is in 1953 with Furtwängler again that Menuhin strayed from that raptly held-back unfolding, with a more flowing and not so deeply-felt 9:23 duration. That's one of the main reasons to prefer Menuhin-Furtwängler 1947 over 1953. 1947 is possibly Menuhin's most beautiful and intense Larghetto, but all is relative: those with Silvestri and Klemperer are beautiful in their own right. The only thing you can say against Klemperer is that there is some artificial spotlighting of the woodwinds: the clarinet at 1:13 sounds as if he were seated next to the soloist. Beautiful, but not realistic.

I can't fault Greenfield on his assertion that Klemperer's Finale is the slowest of the three; in fact, it is, up to the cadenza, the slowest version I've ever encountered, and if the movement's total timing doesn't give that impression, it is only because, as already mentioned, Menuhin plays a vastly abridged version of Kreisler's cadenza. But still, Greenfield WAS crap when he claimed that there, the Silvestri version took "a turn for the better with Menuhin gaining confidence and a genuine spring in the rhythm". It is the opposite in fact, and before Klemperer, Menuhin's version with Silvestri was the second slowest ever that I had encountered after the one of Schneiderhan with Van Kempen in 1953. Even compared to the expansive and mellow Furtwängler in 1953, it was sucked of any spirit of dance, and was the most exceptionable part of the 1960 recording. In fact, I find the still slower Finale with Klemperer preferable: first, because it is, precisely, even more radical: as long as you are going to have the slowest ketchup in the world, have it REALLY the slowest. Second, because that very radicalism of approach serves character much better. Of the Silvestri version, I wrote that "the bounce that Menuhin manages to retain in his phrasings, his sweet lyricism in the more lyrical passages and the vigor of the orchestral outbursts are no real substitute" to the lost siprit of dance. With Klemperer, the massive power, the gruff and clumsy vigor ARE interesting substitutes. It calls for the same kind of comment that I made already about Klemperer's Finale of the Emperor Concerto with Barenboim, or the Scherzo and Finale of his Eroica: it evokes images of one of the hippos of Fantasia musingly hop-scotching, followed by the rest of the posse. It might have been impossibly elephantine and plodding - and it probably is, in fact - yet I don't find it "dull" at all. Whatever you think, it is unique, the Finale of the Violin Concerto as you've never heard it.

I don't know why HMV made this recording in the first place, but I know why they were right to do it: because, where many performers, when they re-record a work, are happy to offer exactly the same interpretation as before (Heifetz or Grumiaux come to mind), Menuhin here offers one that is very different from his previous ones, and one that is highly valuable to boot. In all, I find that the comparison of Menuhin's various recordings, and the observation of how different they are, paints an interesting and unexpected image of the violinist. Not one to impose, like Heifetz or Grumiaux, HIS unchanging views on his different conducting partners, but rather one ready and eager to blend into THEIR views. If the versions with Furtwängler are so Furtwänglerian, if the first movement with Silvestri is so strict in tempo and so expansive and massively powerful with Klemperer and if the Finale with Klemperer sounds so much like Klemperer's other Beethoven, it seems to be because Menuhin arrived at the recording sessions and said, "okay Wilhelm, okay Constantin, okay Otto, how would you like to conduct it? It'll be fine for me, I'll be happy to fit in, and say what I wish to say within your conception, rather than against it". That's a good definition of "the meeting of minds", and of making music together. As long as shelve space permits, I am not parting with this one, and if things get really bad, it's the one I'll keep with Lucerne 1947. Now if things get REALLY bad....

Menuhin-Klemperer's recording was first reissued on EMI's mid-price Studio collection in 1985, with no complement, [[ASIN:B00000DOGQ Beethoven: Violin Concerto in D Major, Op. 61]]. This edition here is from 1992 but it uses the same 1985 transfer. It adds the same Two Violin Romances, recorded with the Philharmonia Orchestra under John Pritchard in 1960, that EMI has also used as a complement to the Silvestri-conducted Concerto in the edition mentioned in the first paragraph, and that way all tastes and preferences are met. There, Menuhin illustrates, with Pritchard as he did already with Furtwängler in 1953 (a recording reissued by Testament as a complement to their 1947 Lucerne Concerto), an approach that is way too solemn and sentimental for the music's own good, and even more in Op. 50 than in Op. 40. Once you've heard Kremer and Harnoncourt's way more flowing approach, there's no turning back ([[ASIN:B000000SG9 Ludwig van Beethoven: Violin Concerto in D major, Op. 61 / Romance in G major, Op. 40 / Romance in F major, Op. 50 - Gidon Kremer / Chamber Orchestra of Europe / Nikolaus Harnoncourt]]). The Romances offer a welcome complement for the Menuhin completist, but it is the Klemperer-conducted Concerto that warrants purchase, with or without complement.

*******************************************************************
And a post-script from August 17: mp3 buyers, BEWARE! First of all, the mp3 download page to which this CD is linked is credited as Menuhin's version with Silvestri, not Klemperer. And anyway, the timings of the three tracks makes it INCOMPATIBLE WITH BOTH

1st movement Silvestri 23:29 Klemperer 24:24 download 23:14 NO WAY
2nd movement Silvestri 10:28 Klemperer 10:23 download 9:51 NO WAY
3rd movement Silvestri 10:49 Klemperer 10:07 dowload 11:09 NO WAY

Who is playing on the download page, I don't know. Its timings are compatible with none of the Menuhin versions I know. Who would play a Finale in a few seconds short of 11:09? This is either VERY slow, or with a very long cadenza.

This is not the first time I encounter these mis-links on Amazon - in fact, another one is provided, precisely, by the CD and purported mp3 download of Menuhin's Beethoven with Silvestri, see my review, and something here is starting to badly smell like scam and wilful deception. This is harmful to the performers' reputation (the opposite really of the famous Joyce Hatto scam: not trying to pass off great versions for those of an obscure performer, but trying to pass off recordings of dubious provenance for those of great artists) and deceitful to customers. I hope Amazon is a victim of this and not a party to it: this download is marketed by a "Hallmark" label and they may be the culprit. But I've had another case recently, with Gould and Bernstein's Beethoven Piano Concerto No. 3 & 4 on Sony's Bernstein Century edition (ASIN B000058TKA), and when I alerted Amazon to a gross mislink with another recording of the same by fifteenth-rate perfomers, I was rudely bullied off by two different interlocutors, so I'm starting to have doubts about the implication of Amazon in this. I've sent them a message to alert them to this one too (see comments), we'll see if there is a pattern.
rows.title: much better than its reputation (me against the critics). MP3 BUYERS, BEWARE!

rows.text:
I enjoyed most of the stories in this anthology! The only reason I gave it 4 stars instead of 5 is because there were some stories included that didn't seem to be finished or didn't flow well; just abruptly ended! Individual Story Ratings Below:

Suddenly Taken: 3.5 🌟 This story was funny, hot and fast paced! I really liked it! I wished it was 2 pov so I could have got a better idea of Kachuck's personality but not a huge issue for me. Good job!

Alien Most Wanted: 4 🌟 This was a good story with pov of view from both the H/h. It had a good storyline, well rounded characters, well paced, and funny side characters! It has a nice steamy scenes 🔥😉. The epilogue seems to build up for a continuing of the series. Will read future works.

Her Pointy Alien: 3.5 🌟 This story includes a curvy h and sweet male 🥰. It had 2 pov, a nice steam scene, and male has an interesting spiky peen! Good read!

Skoar: 3 🌟 This story has a nice storyline, continuing series from author, interesting characters, and I loved the "heartspire" aspect! It was 2 pov and male has interesting splitting peen, and flows well!

Rescued from the Depths: 5 🌟 This was definitely one of my faves of the book! Love anything by Ella Maven! It included an interesting spin from the original series, a weet protective H, and is 2 pov! The H has a very Interesting sucking peen and steamy scenes included! Can't wait for the next!

Ravished by the Escapee: 3 🌟 Interesting take on Alien mates! The story seemed a bit rush but it was funny! It confused me a little with the added bit of the H crossing paths with the h 20 years prior and the h seemed a little juvenile for a 35 year old but didn't take away too much from the story. Interesting peen and hot 🔥 scenes included!

Earth 4040: 3 🌟 I loved that the h was a fighter and the omegaverse element going on! Good characters and good world building. I didnt feel like the alien was very Alien like and wish he would have been better described but overall good read!

Zella Unlocked: 3 🌟 2 pov and good storyline. Loved that the h was so sweet and caring and that our warrior H was a sweet warrior with Wings 🥰 The romance intimance seemed a little slow paced to me but the hot times are 🔥 Very long lasting orgasams!! Didn't care for the cliffhanger. Good read.

Warrior in Chains: 3 🌟 I loved the badass h! It was interesting that the H is half human and half alien and the h is alien! It was fast paced and full of action. I felt like it had a little too much conflict for such a short read but not bad. It was 2 pov with an abrupt ending on sex scene but not bad.

Seized by the Alien Berserker: 4 This was an interesting read. It seemed a little different from other in the series. Loved that the H overcame is natural instinct to use extreme force. It was well written in dual pov with hot 🔥 times. Wished the ending gave a bit more but will be full version later.

Lost Girl: 2.5 🌟 This was a good read. I like anything Fated mates. The H and his people are hunter/gathers and come across the h with a baby. The h has amnesia but knows she must fulfill her mission and get the baby to safety. It lacked some in world building and character description/building but still good read. The steamy times are skipped over in this one 😥 I didn't feel there was good closure and no epilogue.

The Recruit: 3 🌟 This started off really well! I loved the sweet h and strong H! There was some action, nice heat, and good character/world building! I didnt care for the ending and hint of her going back to him rather then her just staying. This was 1 pov and it had no epilogue.

Destination Alien Bride: 5 🌟 Good start! Well written with good characters and good world building! The H was a funny, grumpy alpha male! I love him and the strong willed female! This story has an interesting take on finding your Fated mate and very interesting peen!

Tacori: 4.5 🌟 This story includes the H fighting a mate bond. It has an interesting storyline, well rounded characters and good action/steam! It has interesting side characters. It doesn't have an epilogue but does close well.

Drashar: 3.5 🌟 This story has a sweet caring H and funny, strong willed female! It has good world building and I enjoyed the underwater world. There are 🔥 scenes and interesting peen. It did seem to end abruptly but not bad start. 2 pov

First Warrior: 2 🌟 This story was 1 pov. It was an interesting concept but just seemed rushed and more telling than showing. I didnt feel pulled into the story. I like the symbiont idea. Didn't have any real world building and no epilogue. I skimmed through this book.

Between a Rock Alien and His Hard Place: 2 🌟 It was dual pov and I loved the idea of a rock alien. It was more sex than story to me with very little real interaction between the characters. The sex is hot 🔥 🥵! However I didnt feel the h was special with how our H flirted with any female but I was glad he overcame his instinct. I love that they were forever Mates but not interested in reading heat induced flirting which I get the feeling would happen in future. I did really enjoy the alien that looked like aliens! Would love to see others with a human partner!

Won by the Alien Gladiator: 2.5 🌟 2 pov The story was ok. Not too much happenig but the sex is 🔥 I did like that the H fights his mating instinct so his h feels safe. Very Interesting vibrating peen 🥳🥵

Draco Warlord Her Alien Conqueror: 2 🌟 2 pov with interesting storyline, some good push/pull! Love the dragon aliens! H went from grumpy to sappy. It didn't flow as well in some places as in others. Some funny lol moments but it ended abruptly on a tbc. It didn't feel finished and it didn't have any steam!

Glowsticks: 3.5 🌟 multiple pov/rh. Goodstart on world building and introducing a series! I loved how the 3 H's all had their own personalities and their interesting glowing peen with strawberry tasting 💦! Well read future works!

April in Aurora: 1 🌟 1 pov This was well written and the little there was seemed interesting but it wasn't a full story or novella. No romantic interactions and steam.

Alien Boss: 4.5 🌟 2 pov I loved this angst short story! It has a good storyline and I love the funny push/pull interactions between the characters!

Claimed by the Alien Duke: 1 🌟 2 pov Time traveling alien romance 😍 I was so looking forwarded to reading this story but it just ended after introducing the female and the time traveling aliens looking for mates in the 1700s. I will read a finished version.

Attra Chronicles 1: 2 🌟 multiple povs follows 2 couples: 1 alien couple and 1 time traveling human couple. I couldn't really get into this one. The space/time travel was interesting though. It hinted at steam but didn't follow through and the multiple couples threw me off.

Fated to a Dragon Warrior: 4 🌟 2 pov. This was a sweet short story! I love the Fated mates, the sweet male, the storyline, the sexy times, and the hea!! It seemed to be something but still a really good read!

The Alien's Princess: 2 🌟 2 pov The blurb was promising: a couple together against all odds but it just feel a little flat for me. Wished the author would have been a little more creative with descriptions and world building. It lacked detail and didn't flow well for me.

Her Alien Warrior: 3.5 🌟 2 pov This started a little slow for me probably because of the language barrier! I loved how the H is integrated into the family unit. It was just a little slow with half of the book being about the h and her son caring for the wounded warrior. It was sweet with some steamand no epilogue!

Star Hound: 3 🌟 2 pov I liked the undercover storyline and the empath aspect. Both character are half human/ half alien different species. They didn't really seem alien like and I do prefer alien like aliens but it was a pretty good read! I liked that the romance intertwined with a mystery! Nice steam.

Nebular Nuts: 2. 5 🌟 2 pov I loved that the H has 2 🍆😈 I really liked that the H rescued the h from slavery but I wish there was more action. Steam was hot but rushed. I think it needs some editing and revision.

Her Runed Alien: 4 🌟 2 pov This was a little soft for the Vikingr im used to. I still liked the story a lot. It was interesting and I loved the interactions and steam! I laughed a lot during this short story!

Ice Planet Prison: 4 🌟 2 pov This short story gave me so many feels! Warning there was some betrayal and I hated it! I hated even more that I understood why the H did it! I would have preferred he fought more for her and it didn't feel as special that he didn't actually make her his mate but that he wouldn't mate anyone else. This story is very well written and makes the reader feel things even if they don't want to. Good job!

Rescued by the Alien: 2 🌟 2 pov This was an ok read but it was pretty repetitive and ended abruptly. A good amount of dialogue and telling rather than showing. I didnt feel a connection between the mcs!

Bounty: 3.5 🌟 2 pov. Bad a** female-🥰 It had good world building and descriptions. I loved the characters game of "21 questions " and their interactions! There wasn't a lot of action in the first half but the author still kept my attention. There was no epilogue but it ended well! There was steam but it was as hot as I like to read but that just personal preference! Action picked up in 2nd half but not overdone.

Prince of Rats: 1.5 🌟 2 pov This was well written and I like the enemies to lovers vibes I got from this but it was not a finished romance story! The "male-whore " is one of my least favorite character types but I guess it builds a good opportunity for his character to grow. I would like to read this finished.

The Alien Pirates: 2.5 🌟 2 pov This was interesting story idea but too rushed and unrealistic. It has potential but fell flat!

These reviews are just my personal opinion and meant as no disrespect to any of the authors! All in all this was a really enjoyable anthology!
rows.title: So Many Great Reads 🤩🤩🤩 ⚠️ Long Review

rows.text: Jersey is my favorite of the hw’s franchise. Couldn’t stand Jennifer last season, starting to like her. Teresa’s ego has blown to epic per portion. She has been making a complete jackass out of herself for the past few seasons. Why the f*** do they all kiss her crazy ass so much? Especially Dolores, who doesn’t seem like the ass kissing type. Strange. Margaret is a force. Mucho respect for this bitch. Bought some of her reading glasses at TJMaxx recently. They’re ok, bought them because I love her. Melissa is freakishly beautiful. What’s up with that? She old. Doesn’t look or act it. Joe, her husband is a complete moron. Loves himself. I think Melissa loves herself too. She’s alright tho, bitch can throw back the alcohol like a champ. Get flirty with the gals when trashed. I love Frank Catania. I want him to be my therapist & life coach. Very smart sensible fella with a sketchy past. He’s perfect. I seriously think he could help me want to live. My life sucks. I want to be a rh. All they do is travel around, eating great food, drinking delicious wine, shop, start trouble, cry, meltdown, laugh, hug, then start more shit, but with someone else. It’s great. I highly suggest watching. I’m a esthetician, i service hw’s for a living. They’re better on tv, than in real life.
rows.title: More Table Flippin’!

rows.text:
Damn! How many more go down before you just go completely out of your mind. Sean of course deserved it trying to take Rick's family away. That really didn't bother me so much. Then Lori meets her demise and Rick just loses it and I myself was in despair. Then the despicable Governor who you just could not wait for Michonne to take one of his eyes out. The end of Season 3 saw Andrea finally get knocked off (that one made me shed a few tears and I was pissed.) I wanted her to get out of the weird relationship with the Gov and get back with the group, but every time she did the wrong damn thing, crap! Now Carl is acting even more stranger then ever and your thinking what is going to happen with that kid??? The best part was when Merle finally gets it just one time (Yeah!). He lets Michonne go just when you thought he'd never. Wanted to give that racist redneck one big slap on the back. Before I could he was axed also by our devious no good demented human, The Governor. Now I can't wait for Season 4 if only to see The Governor come back so someone can kill him real slow like. Maybe Michonne wacks off one finger at a time or puts his other eye out. I watched seasons 1-3 over again on Netflix in about 4 days time. Just finished season 3 today. First time around I think I missed about 6 episodes. The best series I have ever seen. Hate it when someone from the first group gets the axe. Sad the way Dale got it (even though sometimes he can get on your nerves, usually he was right though). Carl screwed up by going after a Walker and it got loose and killed Dale later. So many twists and turns. Rick is only human since he makes a lot of mistakes only to make up for most of them later. Way too much to put on one mans shoulders. As he states "It's not me anymore making decisions, it's US". As well it should be. Glen getting beat to hell by Merle and Maggie forced to take her top off by The Governor makes it a non-stop action thriller. Damn! That Governor is one sick SOB. Heads in an aquarium and keep his Walker daughter to try and sooth her. Whew! Hello! How many of you just love this series? Tell us ALL why.

Update: For those that give this series one star. Most of these 80 1 star reviews are because it will not play on their device: Computer, Ipad, TV or whatever. These reviews mean nothing about the show itself. Then the few others that state how horrible The Walking Dead is. Hello! You have over 5,000 5 star and about 20 1 star that dislike the series itself. Usually when a product is reviewed a 10 to 1 ratio means it's usually pretty damn good. In this case taking out the the complaints not about the show itself but can't play it. The ratio is like 250 to one. which means of course that only 1% or less (you always will get a select few that you can not please no matter what. Forget those one percent because they mean next to nothing. This series is the best that has ever been on TV, ever! Read the reviews, Watch the Walking Dead Panels. They are knocking folks DEAD, period! Those are the facts. I and 99+% of the people can not wait for Season 4 (one week to go!). Get your popcorn ready........
rows.title: Season 1-3 The Walking Dead

rows.text:
Viva La Bam is constantly and unfairly being compared to Jackass. They are similar, but in very small ways, and the one that stands out the most is the fact that Bam Margera is in this as well. They don't do the same masochistic stunts you'd find in Jackass. There's rarely ever any forced vomiting or cruelty to animals (like in Wild Boys). What Bam does is torment his surprisingly tolerant parents. This, the first volume, contains the first eight episodes of the show (an entire season) plus a disc of special features:

Episode 1: Phil's Hell Day/Bam's Skate Park: they couldn't have picked a better episode to start the season.

Episode 2: Don't Feed Phil: Bam does everything in his power to deny Phil food for a day.

Episode 3: Family Reunion: Bam's attention turns to his extended family, and you begin to realize that Bam isn't as crazy as his family.

Episode 4: Viva Las Vegas: Rabb gets a mail order bride, and April and Phil celebrate their anniversary. Vito snaps.

Episode 5: Paint Phil Blue & Other Stories: Bam paints Phil, steals April's car, and dig a tunnel into Vito's house.

Episode 6: Very Merry Margera Christmas: Bam celebrates Christmas with friends in the only way he knows how.

Episode 7: April's Revenge: Bam's gets what's coming to him yet April, the person responsible, doesn't realize what she's got herself into.

Episode 8: Scavenger Hunt: Bam and friends tore all of West Chester in order to score points in a crazy scavenger hunt.

The bonus in this collection, though, comes in the special features disc. This includes a heap of deleted scenes which are funnier than anything you can find in the series, if a little more gross at times. It has other things as well, but the deleted scenes are where to be when you pop this into your DVD player.

Though still headed by a bunch of jerks, Viva La Bam isn't nearly as controversial as Jackass or even the CKY Trilogy, though it's equally as funny as both. These DVDs contain some simple laughs that are, at the very least, guaranteed, something that's needed in our world today. Pick this up if you liked Jackass or Wildboys, or are a fan of skateboarding.
rows.title: Dennis the Menace Meets Omen

rows.text:
I'm sitting here, nearly two hours after the credits of Mean Creek have finished rolling, I still feel that sick feeling in the pit of my stomach that won't go away. I can remember sitting there, speechless, as the credits rolled; how I felt light-headed after I left the theater; how I started shaking and my knees started knocking. I can remember clearly that this 89-minute descent into moral hell is the single most disturbing film I've ever seen. And I'm still piecing together how to put any of it into words.

I'll start a couple of years ago, when I read Truman Capote's In Cold Blood and have since never gotten over the idea that it still is very plausible that two men can walk into a home, murder four people in cold blood, and do all of it for virtually nothing. The book scared the crap out of me because of the fact that it happened, things similar to it still happen, and they happen to people just like you and I. I get the chills when thoughts of demons or ghosts or aliens arise, just like everyone else, but what makes me keep the light on at night is the fact that I'm one of millions of people on this earth who are capable of doing anything. And by 'anything,' I don't mean mowing the lawn.

Mean Creek is obstensibly about that dark possibility that lurks within everyone, that one moment when reason is thrown away, the stars are aligned, and disaster strikes. It's really about the aftermath, though; and not just the logistics, but the strange things that we as humans do when we're in a situation that we never imagined we would have to encounter in a million years. And it all begins because Sam (Rory Culkin, officially the best actor in his famous family) is pissed. Pissed because the fat kid, George, beat the crap out of him for basically no reason.

Nobody likes the fat kid, so Sam's older brother, Rocky (Trevor Morgan), and his other friends devise a plan to pay George back. The idea: invite him on a boat trip, get him to play Truth or Dare, strip him naked and get him in the water, and make the fatass walk home embarassed as can be. What actually happens: every single thing that shouldn't.

First of all, George is a nice guy with some issues, and most of the kids in the group decide right quick that the whole plan to ruin him isn't right. Unfortunately, it's a problem with the eldest of the gang, Marty (Scott Mechlowicz), who wants to seek revenge whether George is nice or not. And if you've seen Deliverance (or even the film's enticing trailer for that matter), you know where it's all headed. What writer Jacob Aaron Estes has up his sleeve in the film's masterful denoument, though, is beyond explanation.

Most movies involving kids attempt to strike a cord with its audience where the awareness of innocence wipes clean all sins of a child. Estes' script is so precisely shaped that we don't see redemption from the opening moments; every single scene leading up to the film's climactic moment is accompanied by a stomach-churning tension, a sickening awareness that these kids will not behave like the kids we see in most movies. These kids will behave like adults, desperate for a way out, clawing for a solution, and willing to do anything to achieve it. We cringe when we see George doing and saying genuinely nice things as he's led to the water because the kids are cringing inside. We struggle with what to think of him, just as they do, and then things are turned upside and we're left struggling with what to think of everyone else. The cast, led by Culkin, initially occupy the general stereotypes where we see them fit, but then we see how tragedy and circumstance change them. This mostly unknown cast conveys a feeling of reality that did more than send a chill up my spine.

That feeling of reality will hit you like a ton of bricks. Mean Creek, though tinged with the earthiness of a David Gordon Green film in the beginning, descends into a shocking moral quandary that makes the insidious "Dueling Banjos" of Deliverance seem like a bedtime lullaby. Gone is that cinematic trick of ambiguity (an easy way out for a movie like this), away goes the idea that these kids will persevere, out goes any comfortable solution whatsoever (that the film's solution is so noble is even more sad). Creek is a perfectly plausible set of events that could happen to anyone, and that's what makes it so deeply terrifying.

In its final moments, I fought the urge to look away, Mean Creek is so painful to bear. But it's a necessary pain; we learn from this movie, about moral choice and responsibility, and about the power of social dominance and the consequences of revenge. I wasn't just crushed by this movie; I was so scared I could hardly move, provoked only to silence as the credits rolled. This is real stuff, a movie that reaches a new level of effectiveness in that it burrows into your mind and your heart and puts them both in torment. I was reminded more than a few times of Peter Weir's great Picnic at Hanging Rock during the film, probably because - until now - it was the most disturbing, terrifying film I'd ever seen.

Mean Creek is a towering achievement, but I wouldn't wish it upon anyone. I'll never watch it again, as I enjoy sleeping at night. It's getting an "A" because it's a masterwork, not because it's wistfully romantic like Before Sunset. I'm recommending it because things of this nature happen and we must be faced with them, not because it's fun. A
rows.title: A masterwork - the most disturbing film I've ever seen

rows.text:
Three amazing Jackass flicks in one? How can you possibly go wrong? The price is good too.

I especially enjoyed Jackass 2.5. It may even be better than Jackass 2 with a lot of stuff that couldn't have been shown, were too long, or would've have been, surprisingly, too non-sequitur for a theatre release. All three movies are must-haves for ANYONE who likes to laugh and isn't uptight.

Don't listen to the idiot reviewers that give Jackass videos 1 star based solely on content, they obviously haven't matured enough to look past the initial shock and see the comedic value, ingenuity, art, and camaraderie between all of the lifelong-friends in the film.
rows.title: A total blast!

rows.text:
The Wicked Awakening of Anne Merchant by Joanna Wiebe
Book #2: The V Trilogy
Source: Publisher
My Rating: 5/5 stars
My Review:

** SPOILERS FOR BOOK ONE!!!! **

The Wicked Awakening of Anne Merchant is the second installment of The V Trilogy and unlike most second books in a trilogy, it does not slow down at all. The book opens with a very confused Anne being told by Teddy that she is not only going back to Cania Christy but she is going to have to help him take down not one but two leaders of the evil isle!! Shouldn’t be a problem given the cast of characters she has at her disposal. Here’s the crew:

Teddy: Huh? Who knew there were double agents where Heaven and Hell are concerned? As things stand, Teddy seems to be a fine actor and though he isn’t technically evil, he puts on a good show. Teddy is full of information and though he isn’t around much when he shows up, things get all kinds of interesting.

Hiltop/Mephistopheles: HA!! That creepy little bastard (what’s the word for a girl bastard?) is all kinds of demoted!! Turns out when one of Satan’s minions royally screws the pooch and gets outsmarted by a couple of kids, he loses all his power and followers but doesn’t get to leave the evil isle. Never fear, Hiltop is still on top of her/his game and just because the power and power base have been dramatically reduced doesn’t mean there aren’t plans within plans. Hiltop clearly has it out for Anne but on occasion she/he feels a bit generous and imparts some information.

Dia Voletto: Uugghh!! Talk about creepy wrapped in a pretty package. Dia is the new leader of Cania Christy and with him comes a whole new crew and nearly every one of them is smokin’ hot. Dia is seemingly clueless and only concerned with himself but he is actually quite clever. . . . sometimes. From the moment he lands on the island, Dia sets out to expand his territory and his influence. He launches construction on Cania College and within days of his arrival, pulls Anne into his madness. For a time, Anne is clueless about Dia’s interest in her but once she figures out how to manipulate him, Dia has no hope of survival.

Ben: Oh, Ben!! I’m not at all sure how I feel about Ben at this point. Sure, he loves Anne but then there’s that give it all up and accept my fate attitude that he has going on. I really want to like Ben, I do but when he gives up after everything Anne has gone through, it really pisses me off. Ben only gets truly interesting after Anne works her magic and literally forces him to suck it up and fight for his life. He does have a few endearing moments but for me, Ben had better square his sh*t away for the big finale!

Anne/Miss Saligia: Holy crap, are you kidding me? As if Anne’s life(ish!) weren’t hard enough already now she has to contend with the fact that she was once (still is!) a seriously powerful and seriously sexy devil with a tail. Oh, and she was Dia’s girlfriend. Really?? Once Anne gets something of a handle on her alternate identity and some information regarding Saligia, she begins to use it to her advantage. Within a few months, Anne and Teddy’s mission to overthrown the big bads is well under way and Anne has a serious number of followers. As her power base grows, so does Anne’s strength and confidence. With the help of a few trusted individuals and some serious badassery, Anne is on track to destroy all that Hiltop and Dia have built.

The Bottom Line: Joanna Wiebe just doesn’t follow the rules!! The second book in a trilogy is generally the slower paced, loads of backstory, get ready for the big finale book. (BTW: That is NOT a complaint from me. I love that crap ) The Wicked Awakening of Anne Merchant is anything but and from the moment the book opens to the moment it ends, it is balls to the wall action. Anne is up to her butt in trouble, puzzles, mysteries and drama and if she doesn’t pull it together a lot of people are going to suffer as a result of her schemes. This is an incredibly twisty and turny book but Wiebe has done a fantastic job of leading the reader through each twist and turn with little to no difficulty. Just when you think you have everything figured out and all the players squared away, another twist or turn shows up to throw you off. I LOVED IT!! When you add the backstory of some of the characters and the hints about the third and final book in the trilogy, The Wicked Awakening of Anne Merchant comes in as a really fun and delightfully twisted read.
rows.title: REVIEW CONTAINS SPOILERS!!!!!

rows.text: The Rolling Stones made rock main-stream, transforming it from its bluesy roots to what it is today.  These guys are the original rock 'n rollers, and set the standard for all rock acts from then on.  Everyone like Fred and Bernard are just being trolls.  They know that they are worthless humans-beings, and find joy in making fun of the roots of rock just to piss people off and make themselves feel important.  And apparently the only negative adjectives in their vocabulary are "suck" and "crap".  Wow, "they suck, and are crap", great review, moron. Go to school, learn about the English language, and stop being leeches, you ignorant pricks.  I wouldn't mind if negative reviews are constructive, but when its these one setence blurbs designed to lower the products rating, that just pisses me off.  Hope you get plenty of hate-mail, you earned it.  Anyway, The Stones roll and sure as hell rock the hell out of the music scene, to this day.
rows.title: Stone the Haters

rows.text:
To all those critics of power metal i sincerely hope you die a horrible death. Sorry for that unsavoury attitude but i am just totally pissed at someone,a 'friend', who put me off power metal for a while until i regained my senses and came back to stand strong behind one of my favourite metal genres!

I'll quickly discuss my history with power metal. This album was actually the first power metal album i bought in my life. And i'll tell you straight off i loved every moment of it. Before listening to this i really never knew power metal i mean sure i'd heard of Stratovarius and Helloween but didn't really know them or their sound at all. I had very little concept of what power metal was. I was under the more British and American spell that heavy metal had to be as loud and aggressive as possible so i was mainly into metalcore-ish stuff, death metal and thrash, although i predominantly like thrash metal. To tell you the truth when i was listening to some of the stuff i was then, like Cannibal Corpse (who i slightly despise now) i didn't really like it that much, it was generic to me didn't stand out or nothing, unlike say Slayer or something. I wanted more melody, more clean singing but still maintaining that speed, heavy riffing and great solos. I basically wanted something that was new and progressive with refreshing tunes.

I bought this album and was amazed at the brilliant musicianship showcased by the guitarists notably Mr Luca Turilli, the vocals were beautiful thanks to Fabio Leone's soft touch and soaring abilities, the wonderful keyboard by Alex Strapoli, the drums were insanely fast bordering on blast beats in places due to Alex Holzwarth's precision skill and the orchestra, AN ORCHESTRA!! I NEVER KNEW THAT BANDS WERE COLLABORATING WITH ORCHESTRAS!! This was a great thing for me because one of my other loves is classical music of all sorts. Rhapsody are god damn amazing musically, structurally, lyrically and as people!! All the songs on this album are fantastic and build up to that hell of a finisher the title track epic of 13mins!!! This album helped me spawn quite a healthy power metal collection featuring bands like Primal Fear, Edguy, Sonata Arctica, Gamma Ray, Helloween, Strato and Nocturnal Rites oh and of course Rhapsody!My verdict on the album is that for a power metal fan or some one whole likes epic music this is a MUST HAVE!!SYMPHONY OF THE ENCHANTED LANDS!

Don't want to rant but i want to address the issue of people who criticise power metal. Power metal in my opinion is modern heavy metal that has progressed lightyears since the '80s. Power metal contains all the things about metal that metalheads love. Fast paced heavy riffing, brilliant wild solos, story or fantasy themed lyrics, double bass drumming, licks a plenty, dual guitars, talented singers with trademark voices and lots of energy! Please tell me what else heavy metal is about? It's not just about being loud, noisy and drunk, sure thats fun and there are plenty power metallers that advocate that eg Edguy lol. Power metal bands always and i mean ALWAYS cite the metal greats as their influences Priest, Maiden, Accept, Metallica, Queensryche, Fates Warning, Helloween etc and follow in their footsteps. I gurantee you every power metal band you listen to will make you think 'Boy that sounded like Maiden' or like with Primal Fear 'F*** this is Judas Priest man!!'.. Power metal has an uplifting spirit to it as well and a sense of being a metal community-- often inviting black metal singers,death metal singers and female vocalists to collaborate with them-- refer to Tobias Sammet's master piece the Metal Opera by Avantasia for a great example of this or Kamelot's the Black Halo. And so freakin what if they wear leather and clad and look like a bunch of sissy boys, metal kinda has a history of that if you look back on it Maiden, Priest, Manowar and KISS to name some-- for christ sake look at the back of Number of the Beast they don't look like fat muscle men do they??

My 'friend' as i spoke of earlier begged me to burn her a copy of my Rhapsody cd when i ranted and raved about it. Then when i did and met her again she's like it's really cheesy. Tell me wtf is cheesy about singing with a great voice about a story of Tolkien proportions using heavy metal and adding all these elements like orchestras, narration and SFX to enhance the experience?? NOTHING!! And here's the irony she listens to bands that 'aren't cheesy' like Greenday, Red Hot Chilli Peppers and Pink!!??? What a load of crap man i hate to tell that bitch but 'love' and 'heartache' are the most cheesy and overused cliches in the music industry!!

My final word: POWER METAL IS GREAT!! RHAPSODY ROCK!! And buy this CD no regrets believe me!

Hope that was helpful.

\m/
rows.title: Epic Power Metal... nothing can surpass this

rows.text:
When we were kids Nick scared the baby crap out of us with Birthday Party. We grew up with him and weren't quite as scared because The Bad Seeds' stories were night-time fables told by a benevolent campfire master. Just leave the light on, we'll be OK even if dreaming of being buried under ten feet of snow white snow.
Now we're all grown up. Middle age, actually and Nick's still right here in the room. Maybe he's exorcised his personal demons. Maybe he hopes we have too. Yeah right.
Grinderman wakes us from the complacency of midlife. We stopped bitching and fighting and we started whining. Like old men on the porch. "In my day there were fights at every show." Blah,blah,blah.
But Nick reminds us that we're still pissed off. And it's OK to be pissed. Maybe we've slowed down. But we're not dead. Yet. And neither is Nick.
When we were 21 we had to juggle girls like Naomi Watts juggling for King Kong. OOOps, dropped a few. Now, after break-ups and divorces and rehabs the woman don't seem as ethereal, sweet and horny. Do they? We've got the "No P-ssy Blues" and thank God Nick is still here with us to commiserate. Play it over and over on the way home from the party. Loud. And don't forget to feed the cat before you go to bed. Alone. Thanks Nick for sticking around.
rows.title: Life IS Pain

rows.text: Everyone on here who says " oh buy this album instead cause these guys suck " is crap. I have all of Obituary's albums including Frozen In Time, I have Dissection, Marduk, Amon Amarth, Old Inflames, Dismember, At The Gates. This is not Emo crap, because I hate that crap. This is good melodic poppy metal, much like Edguy, Hammerfall or most of the european power metal scene. This is good straight ahead rock/metal. Out of all the crap today out from America KSE, Shadows Fall, and Unearth ar the best of this melodic AMerican scene. If you like tech thrash from america THen buy Nevermore or Jag Panzer. Which i also own. When Darkness Falls, Rose Of Sharyn, and End of Heartache are the best songs on here. This is a great album for anyone trying to get into metal period because its a starting point to develop your tastes for flashy riffs, straight head groovy riffs, screaming, actual singing, everything. These guys incorporate every aspect of metal into their arsenal and maybe they dont do it the bst but these guys have brought elements of power, thrash, death,black, and melodeath metal and made it poopy at the same time. Its that simple. Their is so much great metal out there and alot of underground stuff from sweden and germany and finland. In order to get into that stuff you have to start with something that will ease you into it. END OF HEARTACHE IS THAT ALBUM.
rows.title: The Final End Of Heartache review. Period PEOPLE!!!!

rows.text:
Unlike the earlier releases on Atlantic (Stay Hard and The Pack is Back), Raven had complete control over the production of Life's a Bitch. There were no record execs pressuring them to sound commercial and "sell, sell, sell". No heavy-handed producer was brought in to take over the project and make them use synthesizers and horn sections (ala The Pack is Back). In fact, Atlantic had all but given up on Raven and pretty much let them do whatever they wanted on their final Atlantic release. The result was quite possibly the best Raven album of their career.

Start to finish, Life's a Bitch shows Raven in peak form. The songs are loud, insane, and catchy. The guitar solos are larger than life. The arrangements are fresh and varied. You have 2-and-half minute slam-fests like "You're a Liar" and epic prog-like rockers like "On the Wings of an Eagle". Of course the usual Raven lunacy is on proud display in "Overload", "Pick Your Window", "Life's a Bitch", and "Playing with the Razor". There honestly is not a weak track on here. No filler, no shortcuts, no disposable over-produced yawners. This is Raven doing penance for the earlier Atlantic releases, which quite frankly really weren't their fault.

You can imagine being young starving musicians, coming to a new country, with a new record deal, and your bosses telling you what to do. Raven was at the mercy of Atlantic and did what they were told and recorded The Pack is Back. But for that, the band was summarily dismissed as sell-outs by the fans and dumped by Atlantic. It's unfortunate, because since then all Raven has done has put out one stellar release after another.

Life's a Bitch is the meaner, wiser, hungrier re-birth of Raven. No fan of NWOBHM metal should miss this. It truly was a landmark recording for this British trio.
rows.title: This is Raven done right

rows.text:
Her latest album, Rebel Heart, fuses a lot of genres but it is a pop album. I own the Super Deluxe edition which features additional songs that are not included on the regular or deluxe versions.
One thing about Madonna's style, especially since 2000 is her fusion of rock, pop, trance, hip hop, and R&B. Rebel Heart is no exception to this. I think this album of hers is her best release since her 2005 album, Confessions on a Dance Floor.

Here are all 23 tracks:
1. Living For Love
2. Devil Pray
3. Ghosttown
4. Unapologetic Bitch
5. Illuminati
6. Bitch I'm Madonna
7. Hold Tight
8. Joan Of Arc
9. Iconic
10. HeartBreakCity
11. Body Shop
12. Holy Water
13. Inside Out
14. Wash All Over Me
15. Best Night
16. Veni Vidi Vici
17. S.E.X.
18. Messiah
19. Rebel Heart
20. Borrowed Time
21. Addicted
22. Beautiful Scars
23. Graffiti Heart

Conceptually, Madonna describes this album as a two-in-one. The name Rebel Heart implies two sides to her persona: the Rebellious Madonna, and the Heartfelt sensitive Madonna. Musically, this album was created with a large number of producers, something Madonna has never done before on an album. Typically, Madonna works with one or two producers per album. The wide range of producers on Rebel Heart created songs that fuse multiple genres together:

Rock n Roll fused with Dance/Pop: The songs that have a guitar-based instrument on which the rest of the EDM and Madonna's lyrics are built, include Addicted, Rebel Heart, Joan Of Arc, Borrowed Time, Grafiti Heart, Devil Pray, Ghosttown, and Body Shop (which uses the Indian Sari instrument). Each of these songs start with the guitar hook. Some have acoustic guitar, and others, like Addicted, have the electric guitar as the central instrument. The producers fuse EDM beats on top of the guitar hooks, and in combination with Madonna's voice creates a rich blended listening experience.

These songs fuse piano bars reminiscent of the days of the Blues, with dance, pop, or R&B: HeartBreakCity, Messiah, Wash All Over Me. These songs are melancholy in nature, sort of like Madonna's version of the Blues, but fused with moderate paced pop drum beats, synthetics, and rich dramatic vocals. These songs produce a different feel from her guitar based tracks.

Her first single from the album, Living for Love, fuses the Gospel genre with pop and dance. It starts out with classic Madonna-esque pop sounds reminiscent of her 80s and 90s era. The track does include a choir of people singing the chorus in the background.

Her song, Unapologetic Bitch fuses a Reggae beat and Hip Hop. The base of the song is in Reggae style, with the Hip Hop beats and sounds added over it. The pace of the song is that of the Reggae style, moderate, not fast.

Her remaining tracks that I did not name, particularly Bitch I'm Madonna, Veni Vidi Vici, Iconic, and S.E.X. are very much Hip Hop and R&B infused tracks. She either raps on these tracks or sings and features guest rappers like Nicki Minaj and Nas.

In terms of Musicology, Madonna's Rebel Heart (Super Deluxe) edition is an excellent album for study because she successfully fuses multiple genres: Rock, Blues, Gospel, Pop, Dance, Hip Hop, R&B, Piano jazz. Additionally, the lyrics to these tracks are all written or co-written by Madonna, and her prowess to write powerful lyrics that captures her essence as a persona is as strong today as it was when she first hit the scene in 1981.
rows.title: Rebel Heart Super Deluxe Edition - Her best work in a decade!

rows.text: As a hot piss enthusiast I was skeptical when I first heard about this album. Could my deep understanding and love for that yellow foaming formula be respectfully and accurately represented in an audio format? Will they allow us to continue to be mocked, or will this music be powerful enough to turn the page toward a hot piss renaissance? Well, Ghandi once said, "Be the change you wish to see in the world," and the Yellow River Boys have taken it upon themselves to be that change. No longer will myself and my fellow piss drinking cohorts stand in the shadows and watch our hopes go down the drain. At the piss trough we will proudly ask , "Are you going to finish that?" So raise your dog bowl for the Yellow River Boys, and slurp it up.
rows.title: On a Serious Note

rows.text:
I don’t think I knew of one person who actually watched Nahnatchka Khan’s Don’t Trust the B---- in Apartment 23 (streaming on Netflix), and man, y’all don’t know what you were missing. This show is flat-out hysterical. Who knew James Van der Beek was so good at deadpan humor? Part of the reason why he is so funny is because he’s playing (a caricature of?) himself. But the show isn’t really about him. It’s about, of course, the bitch in apartment 23 and her roommate-for-the-moment, June.

So, when you first meet Chloe (a.k.a. “The Bitch”, a.k.a. Krysten Ritter [Breaking Bad]), she’s interviewing candidates to become her new roommate. She settles on June (Dreama Walker - Gran Torino), a girl from the midwest who comes to New York to blah, blah, blah - it’s not important. What is important, however, are two things: a) June is about to get married and b) Chloe tries to sabotage her relationships with her roommates.

In the spirit of the former point aforementioned, Chloe has sex with June’s fiance on the latter’s birthday...on her birthday cake...to prove to June that her fiance is a cheating bastard. Not sure what would get someone so turned on as to have sex with such a cad, but then, considering Chloe, she probably gets off on the sheer fun of ruining someone’s life - ironically, not June’s but her fiance’s.

By doing something this, er, “nice” for June, Chloe ends up forming an unlikely friendship with June - one that reminds us of the girls in high school who would be completely out of our league but befriend us anyway, thereby either ramping up or questioning our true opinions about ourselves.

Cut to James van der Beek, with whom Chloe just happens to be friends. Well, they once dated, but his penis was too big, so they had to break up and now they’re just buddies. Which you’ll find yourself questioning why someone like Van der Beek (in character) would have so much time to spend with this one girl as his buddy, but shut up, because that’s television.

Several jokes arise about Van der Beek’s tenure playing Dawson on The WB network’s (before it became the CW) Dawson’s Creek, and all of them are hysterical, including his attempts at teaching an acting class where his pupils only want to hear his “don’t go” speech to Joey from Season One.

Other side characters worthy of note include Luther (Ray Ford), Van der Beek’s effeminate personal assistant and Mark (Eric Andre), June’s would-have-been manager at the mortgage company where she was supposed to work who now works with her at the coffee shop and who seems to have a treasure trove of comedic talent buried just beneath the surface.

Still others are Robin (Liza Lapira), one of several of Chloe’s formerly jilted roommates (the only difference is, Robin has an unhealthy obsession for Chloe and lives only a couple of apartments away) and Eli (Michael Blaiklock), the creepy horndog neighbor to June and Chloe who’s never further than a window away.

June is adorable without crossing over into annoying, Chloe is that bitch that you’ve still always been drawn to, and Van der Beek is that shallow celebrity that you still kinda wish was your best friend/lover who’d treat you bad. Don’t Trust the B---- in Apartment 23 was a brilliant show that, sadly, only lasted two seasons and had a brand of humor reminiscent of that of Community. Perhaps that’s why ABC “couldn’t find a spot for it” either.
rows.title: The Good Stuff Always Gets Cancelled.
